ref: And so, I'll put you under a curse. Because you killed Abel and made his blood run out on the ground, you will never be able to farm it again.
text: Ɔƒɛni ni, ewida asiaku-ɔ. Na kalɔ kammɔ kéni-ɔ ka ádu ƒ'ɔnwani na nwuna ubugya úwi kalɔ kammɔ lɛlɔ ɔsu.
translated: The woman said, "I am going to the farm." She took her hoe and went to the farm. She worked hard on the farm.

ref: Again, God said to Noah and his sons:
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, Katɔti ɔ́su Noa ku nwuna bebi ɔnini,
translated: The Lord said to Noah, "This is the covenant between me and you and every living creature with you.

ref: Then they struck blind everyone in the crowd, and none of them could even find the door.
text: Na báya na ogya ɔdi ɔ́bwɛ bekyuli bani ƒui nnye keyi mmɔ anumi na básɔ bátanu kayakun na beduo ɔtɔ ɔmmɔ kemmo.
translated: The old woman went to the farm to harvest cassava. She harvested a lot of cassava and brought it home. She prepared it and ate it with her children.

ref: Jacob set up a large rock, so that he would remember what had happened there. Then he poured wine and olive oil on the rock to show that it was dedicated to God,
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, Yakob úti lɛtabi lɛdi únyene aani lɛkpa lɛdi ute Unwoti Katɔti na kesii kammɔ kalɛ na utaakali lɛla lɛni nálɛ mmɔ. Na úti ntɛ bɔdi úwi lɛtabi lɛmmɔ lɛlɔ. Lɛni kani na úti nnɔmii bɔdi úwi-ni lɛlɔ.
translated: Once upon a time, Jacob went to the forest to hunt for animals. He went to the place where Unwoti Katɔti lived and asked him for help in hunting. Unwoti Katɔti agreed to help him. He gave him a magic arrow and told him to shoot it at any animal he wanted to catch. Jacob thanked him and went back to the forest.

ref: All of these clans descended from Esau, who was known as Edom.
text: Bekyuli bani anyi na búnu mmɔ ndi baga bani nálɛ bási Esau, ɔni baalɔ lɛnɔ banii Edom, kaba bebi akɔ.
translated: Bekyuli, the firstborn son of Anyi, was a skilled hunter. He was the father of Esau, who was also a skilled hunter. Esau was the ancestor of the Edomites.

ref: So God created humans to be like himself; he made men and women.
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu Katɔti údie ukyuli na ɔ́lɛ-nwu na nwuna udu kenye. Údie-ma, na badi ɔnana ku uleku.
translated: The old woman Katɔti was very sick and weak. She was unable to get up from her bed. Her children were worried about her.

ref: He was sorry that he had made them,
text: Na úduo Unwoti ka údie ukyuli ɔ́kya kalɔ lɛlɔ. Na nwuna kayi kénu ɔlɛ geen.
translated: The Unwoti people are known for their beautiful and colorful clothing. They also have a unique way of making their hair.

ref: I will give you a son, who will be called Ishmael, because I have heard your cry for help. And someday I will give you so many descendants that no one will be able to count them all.
text: Na ɔ́sɔ ɔ́su-nwu lɛnɔ ɔnini, “Ada kaƒɔ ɔƒaani, na adubo ɔnanabi bɔwɔ. Na adubo-nwu lɛnyi bɔsia anii Ismael. Ɔsika Unwoti únu ƒula kɔnyana kɔni na eewulo.
translated: Ada said that she would go to the farm, and she would bring back some yams. She said that she would give some yams to Ismael. Unwoti, her husband, said that he would go with her.

ref: Later, Leah had a daughter and named her Dinah.
text: Na uwi ɔdi kama ni, Lea úbawɔ ulekubi úte Yakob na ɔ́sia-nwu lɛnyi ɔnii Dina.
translated: When Leah saw that she had stopped bearing children, Leah took her maidservant Zilpah and gave her to Jacob as a wife.

ref: Jacob said, “I have seen God face to face, and I am still alive.” So he named the place Peniel.
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ni, Yakob ɔ́sia mmɔ lɛnyi ɔnii Peniel, na ɔ́su ɔnini, “Lɛ́sia mɛni lɛnyi lɛni, ɔsika lɛ́nu Unwoti Katɔti anumi ku anumi na útayila-mi ngba.”
translated: One day, Jacob was wrestling with a man at Peniel, and he said, “Let me go, for the dawn is breaking.”

text: Bebuo bani nákwii mɛni béƒie bani nélie lɛmwi mmɔ bébweli. Na ka ɔ́nu lɛla lɛmɛni ni, Ƒarao úkeyi lɛlɔ ɔ́gyinya.
translated: The old man went to the farm to harvest cassava. When he arrived at the farm, Pharaoh saw him.

ref: Let's go down and confuse them! We'll make them speak different languages, and they won't be able to understand each other.
text: Anwuɔsu bɛya na bɔyu busie-ma kudɔ. Na butéƒu bana lɛdi lɛni na baatɛmi kalɛ na ɔdi ɔsɔ ulenu ɔkpanadi butemi kalɔ.”
translated: The sun has risen and the birds are singing. The children are playing and laughing, and the elders are sitting and talking.

ref: When you were angry with me and your chief cook, you threw us both in jail in the house of the captain of the guard.
text: Uwi ɔdi úbavi ɔni na awɔ bula ati Ƒarao ákɛ kɔnyi ákya abu ƒula kabla bablane. Ɔmmɔ ɔsu bákya ami ku ƒula bodobodo ɔblane unengu ɔtɔ na ƒula bagyumanane ƒui unengu kesii.
translated: The man went to the farm to work and Pharaoh came and saw him working diligently. He was amazed at the man's strength and asked him to come and work for him in the palace.

ref: “No, you won't!” the snake replied.
text: Amu na kɔlakpi kɔmmɔ kɔ́su-nwu kɔnini, “Kudii! Ɔɔɔdi bukpi na bɛdubo bukpi.
translated: He said to the small children, "Quiet! Don't make noise, don't cry."

ref: His brothers asked, “Do you really think you are going to be king and rule over us?” Now they hated Joseph more than ever because of what he had said about his dream.
text: Mmɔ ni, nwuna banwani béye-nwu banini, “Bɔkali na ada anii awɔ ndubo-bu lɛlɔ butaadi?” Ɔmɛni ɔsu bukisi bɔmmɔ bɔ́kɔ katɔ búbu na kɔna kɔni na úti ɔ́su-ma ɔsu.
translated: Mother said to her daughter, "Where did you get this beautiful cloth?" She said, "I bought it from the market."

ref: Jacob replied, “I am Esau, your first-born, and I have done what you told me. Please sit up and eat the meat I have brought. Then you can give me your blessing.”
text: Na Yakob údie kenye ɔnini, “Ami, ƒula ubi unengudi Esau, na ɔdi. Na lɛla lɛni na anii mbla ni, lɛ́bla kalɛ kani na ásu-mi. Buvele, ta lie, na akun lɛna lɛni na ńtábui líboku-ɔ kalɛ na ete-mi ƒula kusɛ kusɛ.”
translated: Jacob said to his father, “I am Esau your firstborn. I have done just as you told me; please sit up and eat of my game, that you may bless me.”

ref: At sunset he stopped for the night and went to sleep, resting his head on a large rock.
text: Ka útédu kesii kadi ni, ɔƒɛ úbasa-nwu, anwuɔsu ɔ́bla kasusu ka ɔdubɔtɛ mmɔ na kadi kégye. Amu na úti lɛtabi lɛdi ntɛ mmɔ na ɔ́sia lili nina lɛlɔ na ɔ́tɛ ɔlɛɛ.
translated: When the sun rises in the morning, the rooster crows, announcing the day has begun. Then, people wake up from their sleep and go about their daily activities.

ref: While Abraham was still alive, he gave gifts to the sons of Hagar and Keturah. He also sent their sons to live in the east far from his son Isaac, and when Abraham died, he left everything to Isaac. The Death of Abraham
text: Nsu na nwuna ngba kemmo ni, úte nwuna bebi bani na beleku bamba báwɔ béte-nwu ƒui ɔdi biala nwuna kadikɔ na údie-ma ulu na úbu Isak lɛti pɛ.
translated: The Lord saw that the people were wicked, so he regretted making them. He decided to wipe them out from the face of the earth. But Noah found favor in the eyes of the Lord.

ref: He then kissed Rachel and started crying because he was so happy.
text: Ka ɔ́wɔlɔ ni, ɔ́kya Rahel kɔgbu na úse kalɔ uuwulo momoomo.
translated: When the sun rose, Rahel went to the river to wash her clothes.

ref: The Lord said to Abram, “I brought you here from Ur in Chaldea, and I gave you this land.”
text: Na Unwoti ɔ́sɔ ɔ́su-nwu ɔnini, “Ami ndi Unwoti Katɔti ɔni nákpanku-ɔ ɔ́siku Ur ɔma kemmo na Kaldea kalɔ lɛlɔ, kalɛ na liti kalɔ kamɛni lite-ɔ na kenwini awɔ kadi.”
translated: “I am Unwoti Katɔti, the one who brought the light from Ur, the great city of the Chaldeans, to illuminate the world.”
